---
title: "Ch 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables"
subtitle: "Every line is an infinite list of solutions"
lesson-id: "cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 4
ncert-chapter-title: "Linear Equations in Two Variables"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse8.linear-equations-one-var", "cbse9.coordinate-geometry"]
unlocks: ["cbse10.pair-linear-equations", "cbse11.straight-lines"]
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 40
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you solve $3x - 7 = 8$ for $x$? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse8.linear-equations-one-var](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you plot $(2, 3)$ and $(-1, -2)$ on the coordinate plane? *(If no,
   revisit [cbse9.coordinate-geometry](../03-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd).)*
3. Do you know that $2 \cdot 3 + 5 = 11$ in your head?

Three yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

You sell pencils and pens. Pencils cost ₹2; pens cost ₹5. You collected
₹20 today. **How many pencils and how many pens did you sell?**

There is no single answer. Try a few combinations.

```{ojs}
viewof pencils = Inputs.range([0, 10], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "pencils"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof pens = Inputs.range([0, 4], {value: 2, step: 1, label: "pens"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const total = 2*pencils + 5*pens;
  const ok = total === 20;
  return html`<div style="font-family: monospace; font-size: 1.05rem; padding: 0.4rem 0;">
    2·${pencils} + 5·${pens} = ${total}
    ${ok ? '<span style="color:#080"> — exactly ₹20 ✓</span>' : `<span style="color:#a40"> — off by ${total - 20}</span>`}
  </div>`;
}
```

Try $(0, 4)$, $(5, 2)$, $(10, 0)$. All work. Plot each as a point on
graph paper. Notice they lie on a straight line.

## Wonder

1. How many pencil-and-pen combinations make ₹20 exactly? List a few.
2. If you allowed half-pencils ($x$ can be a fraction), how many
   solutions would there be?
3. The combinations you wrote down land on a straight line. Why a
   *line* — why not a curve, or a scattered cloud?
4. If you change the price of a pen from ₹5 to ₹10, what changes about
   the picture you drew?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q1 already shows the lesson: **infinitely many** solutions, not one. Q3 is
the deep question — the *Discover* sequence will force the answer.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1

The equation $x + y = 7$ has many solutions. Find six of them by listing
$x$ from $0$ to $5$ and computing $y$ each time.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$y = 7 - x$. Plug in $x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(0, 7), (1, 6), (2, 5), (3, 4), (4, 3), (5, 2)$. There are infinitely
many: $x$ can be any real number.
</details>

### Problem 2

Plot the six points from Problem 1 on a coordinate plane. What pattern
do they form?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use a ruler. Place the dots carefully.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
They lie on a single straight line. The line slopes downward (as $x$
grows, $y$ shrinks). Every solution of $x + y = 7$ corresponds to a point
on this line; every point on this line is a solution.
</details>

### Problem 3

For the equation $2x + 3y = 12$, find three solutions and plot them.
Verify by ruler that they are collinear (lie on one line).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pick easy values: $x = 0$, then $y = 0$, then any third value.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$x = 0 \Rightarrow y = 4$. $y = 0 \Rightarrow x = 6$. $x = 3 \Rightarrow
y = 2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(0, 4), (6, 0), (3, 2)$. Place a ruler edge against any two — the third
lands on it. So the three are collinear, and indeed every solution of
$2x + 3y = 12$ lies on this single line.
</details>

### Problem 4

Find the solution of $x + y = 7$ for which $x = y$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute $y = x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x + x = 7 \Rightarrow x = 3.5$. Solution: $(3.5, 3.5)$. The line $y = x$
crosses $x + y = 7$ at this single point.
</details>

### Problem 5

Sketch the graphs of $y = 3$ and $x = -2$ on the same plane. Where do
they meet?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$y = 3$ means $y$ is always $3$ — every point at height $3$. What does
that look like?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = 3$ is a **horizontal line** through height $3$. $x = -2$ is a
**vertical line** through $x = -2$. They meet at $(-2, 3)$.
</details>

### Problem 6

A toy seller sells two kinds of toys, $x$ small and $y$ big, with prices
₹3 and ₹4. Total revenue is ₹24. Write the equation; find three solutions
in non-negative integers.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$3x + 4y = 24$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Try $y = 0, 3, 6$ (so that $4y$ is divisible into $24$).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x + 4y = 24$. Solutions: $(8, 0), (4, 3), (0, 6)$. (Other rational
solutions exist, but only these three are non-negative integers.)
</details>

You discovered: a linear equation in two variables has infinitely many
solutions; the solutions form a straight line; horizontal and vertical
lines $y = c$ and $x = c$ are the simplest cases.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **linear equation in two variables** is any equation that can be
written
$$ax + by + c = 0,$$
where $a, b, c$ are real numbers and $a, b$ are not both zero.

A **solution** of this equation is a pair $(x, y)$ that makes the
equation true. There are **infinitely many** solutions.

The graph of every linear equation in two variables is a **straight
line**, and conversely, every straight line is the graph of some linear
equation. Each point on the line is a solution; each solution sits on the
line.

Special cases:

- $y = c$ is the **horizontal line** at height $c$.
- $x = c$ is the **vertical line** at horizontal position $c$.

:::

A short verse:

> *One equation, two unknowns —*
> *Pairs that fit form a line, no more, no less.*
> *Pick an $x$, the $y$ is forced;*
> *Slide along the line, the equation never breaks.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Draw the graph of $3x + 4y = 12$. Find where it cuts the two axes.

A competent solver thinks:

1. **Find the $x$-intercept** ($y = 0$): $3x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 4$.
   Point $(4, 0)$.
2. **Find the $y$-intercept** ($x = 0$): $4y = 12 \Rightarrow y = 3$.
   Point $(0, 3)$.
3. **A check point** at $x = 4 - 4 = 0$? Already used. Try $x = -4$:
   $3(-4) + 4y = 12 \Rightarrow 4y = 24 \Rightarrow y = 6$. Point
   $(-4, 6)$.
4. Plot the three. Lay a ruler — they are collinear. Draw the line.

**Answer:** Line through $(4, 0)$ and $(0, 3)$. $x$-intercept $= 4$,
$y$-intercept $= 3$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Write $5x = 3y - 7$ in the standard form $ax + by + c = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x - 3y + 7 = 0$. Here $a = 5, b = -3, c = 7$.
</details>

**W2.** Is $(2, 1)$ a solution of $4x - 3y = 5$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute and check.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4(2) - 3(1) = 8 - 3 = 5$ ✓. Yes.
</details>

**W3.** Find two solutions of $x - 2y = 6$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = 0 \Rightarrow x = 6$, so $(6, 0)$. $x = 0 \Rightarrow y = -3$, so
$(0, -3)$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Draw the graph of $y = 2x - 1$. Find $y$ when $x = 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Make a table for $x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Table: $(0,-1), (1, 1), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 7)$. Points line up. At
$x = 4$, $y = 7$.
</details>

**P2.** A two-digit number's tens digit is twice its units digit. The sum
of the digits is $9$. Write a system of equations and solve it.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let tens digit $= x$, units digit $= y$. $x = 2y$ and $x + y = 9$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Substituting: $2y + y = 9 \Rightarrow y = 3$, $x = 6$. **Number: 63.**
</details>

**P3.** The cost of a notebook is twice the cost of a pen. Write an
equation in two variables to represent this. Find three solutions.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let the pen cost ₹$y$ and the notebook ₹$x$. Then $x = 2y$, i.e.
$x - 2y = 0$. Solutions: $(2, 1), (10, 5), (20, 10)$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A line passes through $(2, 5)$ and $(4, 11)$. Find a linear
equation that the line satisfies.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try $y = mx + c$. Substitute both points to get two equations in $m, c$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$5 = 2m + c$ and $11 = 4m + c$. Subtract.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$11 - 5 = 4m - 2m \Rightarrow 6 = 2m \Rightarrow m = 3$. From $5 = 2(3) +
c$, $c = -1$. So $y = 3x - 1$, or $3x - y - 1 = 0$.
</details>

**S2.** The taxi fare in a city is structured: ₹15 for the first km, then
₹8 per km. Let $x$ be the total kilometres travelled and $y$ the total
fare. Write the equation relating $x$ and $y$ (for $x \ge 1$). Find the
fare for $x = 10$ km.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First km is ₹15. Each km after that is ₹8. So total $= 15 + 8(x - 1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = 15 + 8(x - 1) = 8x + 7$ for $x \ge 1$. Or $8x - y + 7 = 0$. At
$x = 10$: $y = 8(10) + 7 = ₹87$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The graph of $x = 4$ is a:
(a) point (b) line parallel to $x$-axis (c) line parallel to $y$-axis
(d) line through origin.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = 4$ is the vertical line through $x = 4$. **(c).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Express $y$ in terms of $x$ for the equation
$2x + 5y = 10$, then find $y$ when $x = -5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Solve for $y$: $5y = 10 - 2x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = \tfrac{10 - 2x}{5} = 2 - \tfrac{2x}{5}$. At $x = -5$:
$y = 2 - (-2) = 4$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** The cost of $5$ kg apples and $3$ kg
oranges is ₹240. Write the equation in two variables. If apples cost
₹$x$/kg and oranges cost ₹$y$/kg, find the cost of oranges when apples
cost ₹30/kg.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$5x + 3y = 240$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x + 3y = 240$. With $x = 30$: $150 + 3y = 240 \Rightarrow y = 30$.
**Oranges cost ₹30/kg.**
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Draw the graph of the equation $x + 2y = 6$.
From the graph, find the value of $y$ when $x = -2$, and verify your
answer by substitution.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find at least three solutions, plot, draw the line.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Easy choices: $x = 0 \Rightarrow y = 3$. $x = 6 \Rightarrow y = 0$.
$x = 2 \Rightarrow y = 2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Table.** $(0, 3), (6, 0), (2, 2)$. Plot and draw the straight line.
**From the graph,** at $x = -2$, the line is at height $y = 4$.
**Verify:** $-2 + 2(4) = -2 + 8 = 6$ ✓. Therefore $y = 4$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 4, Problem 4.3
(verifying solutions, similar to W2). **Previous-year pointer:** CBSE
Class 9 sample paper "draw the graph of $ax + by = c$ and read off a
point" (similar to 5-mark above).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"A linear equation in two variables has one solution."** No — it
   has *infinitely many*. A *pair* of linear equations (Class 10) is what
   typically pins down a single solution.
2. **"$y = 5$ is just a number, not an equation."** It is an equation in
   two variables (the coefficient of $x$ is zero). Its graph is a
   horizontal line.
3. **"Equations like $\tfrac{1}{x} + y = 2$ are linear."** No — division
   by $x$ is not linear. *Linear* means $x$ and $y$ each appear only to
   the first power, and not multiplied together.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you take any equation $ax + by + c = 0$ and plot its graph by
   finding three solutions?
2. Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
3. Could you write a linear equation that fits a real-world situation
   (e.g. taxi fare) without prompting?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Euclid's Geometry**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* and *Apply*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse8.linear-equations-one-var`](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)
  — solving $ax + b = 0$.
- [`cbse9.coordinate-geometry`](../03-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd) — the
  $(x, y)$ plane.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.pair-linear-equations`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/03-pair-linear-equations/index.qmd)
  — solving two equations together (intersection of lines).
- [`cbse11.straight-lines`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/12-straight-lines/index.qmd)
  — slopes, angles, distance from a point.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 4 — Linear Equations in Two Variables** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 4.1–4.3: linear equations,
  solutions, graph.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different angle):
  [`t2.linear-functions-graphs`](../../tier-2-algebra1/15-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd).
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / Operations.** "Linear programming" — the science of
optimising under linear constraints — is built entirely on equations of
this form. Modern logistics, scheduling, and supply chains run on these
equations every minute of every day.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Linear regression fits the *best* line through a cloud of
data points; that line is exactly the graph of a linear equation in two
variables. The neural networks behind ChatGPT use the same affine maps as
their fundamental building block, just stacked thousands deep.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the pencil-and-pen Touch is a
  living equation), Russian problem-first (six Discover problems sweep
  from "list solutions" to "all solutions form a line"), Bhāskara
  (compact verse mnemonic).
:::
